# Questions in Livestream 163

Live Stream #163: Succeeding at Failed Questions

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IKcic5xC9fA?t=467" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">7:47</a> | Who are the coastal nations in Genesis 10:5? | He says they are the Mediterranean coastland peoples, especially around the northern Mediterranean, not specifically the Philistines. | He reads the Genesis 10 context around Javan, Elishah, Tarshish, Kittim, and Dodanim, and explains that the passage is naming peoples and places tied to the Mediterranean basin. The "coastlands" are therefore the coastlands of the Mediterranean, especially the northern coast, rather than a narrow reference to the Philistines. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IKcic5xC9fA?t=576" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">9:36</a> | When someone sent a message by Morse code on a telegraph, how did they control who received it? | He says Morse was an open code, so routing depended on operators, call signs, and relay practice rather than secrecy. | He says Morse code was meant to be open rather than encrypted, so anyone with access to the signal could read it. For wired telegraphy, the receiving operator would copy the message and it would then be delivered onward, so delivery depended on the operators and routing system, not on the code itself hiding the recipient. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IKcic5xC9fA?t=930" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">15:30</a> | Where did the modern idea of the mummy's curse come from? | He says it grew out of British newspaper sensationalism around the Tutankhamun discoveries. | He connects the myth to British press coverage of Howard Carter's Tutankhamun excavation. Carter had an exclusive arrangement with the Times, and Dr. Falk says another paper, unable to get access, retaliated by popularizing the curse story; in his telling, it was a press invention rather than an ancient Egyptian theological idea. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IKcic5xC9fA?t=1112" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">18:32</a> | Is there a real natural phenomenon behind the east wind in Exodus 14:21? | He says yes and points to the documented wind-setdown phenomenon. | He identifies the relevant phenomenon as a wind setdown, where an east wind lowers and pushes back water. He says this has been observed in lakes, the eastern Nile region, and even coastal contexts, and he treats it as a well-documented meteorological phenomenon that fits the Exodus language about an east wind. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IKcic5xC9fA?t=1252" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">20:52</a> | Why is David's census sinful, and why does one passage mention Satan while another mentions God? | He says the census reflects a deeper trust and power problem, while the texts present divine sovereignty and a proximate adversarial agent together. | He explains that ancient censuses were commonly tied either to taxation or to military mobilization, and David's was aimed at measuring capacity for further war after God had indicated enough conquest had been done. On Satan and God, he frames the difference through primary and secondary causation: God remains sovereign, while the adversarial agent is the nearer instrument in the narrative. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IKcic5xC9fA?t=1652" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">27:32</a> | Was the reported Ramesses II sarcophagus discovery really major news? | He says some finds are real, but the reporting is more sensational than the underlying discovery. | He notes that Egyptian kings had multiple sarcophagi, not just one, and pieces of royal sarcophagi can be reused, scattered, or already known in museums. He treats the find as plausible but not necessarily sensational, then explains that multiple coffins, statues, and images functioned as redundant proxy bodies for the deceased in Egyptian mortuary belief. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IKcic5xC9fA?t=1936" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">32:16</a> | Will Christian elected officials go to hell if they protect or expand legal abortion? | He treats it as a repeated pastoral question and says persistent deliberate sin reflects being elsewhere in relation to God. | He answers pastorally by focusing on the questioner's repeated attempt to find a permissible line. In his framing, salvation involves siding with God against sin; an official who knowingly protects or expands what he calls the unlawful killing of the unborn is siding with sin for political ambition, which places that person outside the posture of agreement with God that grace presupposes. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IKcic5xC9fA?t=2460" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">41:00</a> | Where do Abraham and Sarah quote laws to each other? | He says it happens often in Genesis and gives examples rather than an exhaustive list. | He gives two examples involving Hagar: Code of Hammurabi sections 146-147 and Lipit-Ishtar section 25. In his reading, Abraham and Sarah paraphrase and selectively apply Mesopotamian legal material about a slave woman bearing children, using the letter of those laws to force Hagar out while ignoring their spirit. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IKcic5xC9fA?t=2850" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">47:30</a> | How should Christians weigh the needs of the many against the needs of the individual? | He rejects the forced either-or and says Scripture makes room for both concerns. | He says Scripture recognizes both individual and communal rights, responsibilities, and guilt, so the answer is balance rather than choosing one side absolutely. He cites examples such as corporate judgment on churches in Revelation and the Old Testament ritual for an unsolved murder near a town, then says these questions must be handled case by case. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IKcic5xC9fA?t=3129" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">52:09</a> | How do you use a squat toilet? | He gives practical travel advice and warns that Westerners often struggle because they have not developed the habit of squatting. | He explains that a squat toilet is literally a hole or fixture one squats over rather than sits on, and warns that Westerners often lack the balance and muscles developed by people who learn to squat from childhood. He adds travel advice for Egypt, saying most tour stops should have Western toilets but some sites, such as the Tannis visitor center, may not. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IKcic5xC9fA?t=4721" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:18:41</a> | In Joshua's long day, where is the requirement that the moon remain in the sky? | He says there is no such requirement and treats the omen framing as neither for nor against Joshua. | He argues that the language in Joshua 10 is omen language about the sun and moon standing in conjunction, not a requirement that the moon remain visibly fixed in the sky. In his reading, the omen is not for or against Joshua; it is a bad omen for the Amorites, and Joshua asks God to use that moment against them. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IKcic5xC9fA?t=5455" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:30:55</a> | In the divorce passage, is the indecency current or past? | He says the passage is not clear and could plausibly refer to either. | He says the passage is ambiguous and could be read either way, but Christ's comment about hardness of heart may imply a past indecency that the husband refuses to forgive. He suggests the indecency involves some kind of sexual or ritual shame, then explains that once the woman remarries another man, the first husband cannot take her back. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IKcic5xC9fA?t=5660" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:34:20</a> | Did a man have to personally write a divorce certificate, and could illiterate men divorce? | He says scribes or hired writers could prepare documents, so literacy was not an impediment. | He says the man was the party who had to issue the certificate in Israelite society, unlike Egypt where either party could initiate divorce. But he adds that scribes and hired writers were available, so an illiterate man could still have the document prepared for him. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IKcic5xC9fA?t=6146" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:42:26</a> | Why did God not simply tell Moses to circumcise his son? | He says Moses already knew the covenant obligation, so God was not giving new information. | He says God did not need to tell Moses something Moses already knew. Moses knew the covenant obligation and had himself been circumcised, so the problem was not ignorance; for reasons the text does not explain, Moses had resisted doing it until Zipporah acted. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IKcic5xC9fA?t=6268" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:44:28</a> | Were Midianites descended from Abraham, and how did they relate to Ishmaelites? | He says some were, but Ishmaelites and Midianites are overlapping rather than identical categories. | He describes the categories as overlapping circles rather than synonyms. Some Midianites were Ishmaelites and some Ishmaelites were Midianites, but later Midianites could also come from other Arabian tribal groups, and some descendants of Abraham may simply not have preserved the Abrahamic practice. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IKcic5xC9fA?t=6377" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:46:17</a> | If Moses was circumcised, why did his wife throw the foreskin at his feet? | He says the issue was their son's circumcision and Moses' failure to observe the covenant obligation. | He clarifies that the foreskin was from their son, not Moses, and says Zipporah threw or dropped it toward Moses' feet rather than physically placing it on him. He reads her action as anger at having to do what Moses should have done and says the text does not explain why Moses was so hesitant. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IKcic5xC9fA?t=6560" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:49:20</a> | Could water imagery in biblical narrative point toward living water? | He says that is a good observation and may form a typology leading toward living water. | He affirms the observation and says the recurring water imagery could form a typology. He specifically connects the pattern to the idea of living water that is realized in the ministry of Jesus Christ, while keeping the answer brief and tentative. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IKcic5xC9fA?t=6624" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:50:24</a> | Why is Christianity not henotheistic if Judaism was? | He says Judaism was not henotheistic but monarchal monotheistic, and the categories should not be confused. | He distinguishes henotheism from monarchal monotheism: henotheism has multiple gods that are ontologically equal or nearly equal while one is worshiped, whereas monarchal monotheism has one supreme, independent God and lesser dependent powers beneath him. He says official Judaism was monarchal monotheistic, even with two-powers theology, and Christianity inherited that framework. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IKcic5xC9fA?t=6951" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:55:51</a> | How does Dr. Falk assess the theory that Isaiah is a Jewish reworking of Greek or Latin literature from Josiah? | He rejects the framing and treats it as an implausible reconstruction. | He says the theory lacks the evidence needed to overturn the usual first-temple dating of at least First Isaiah. He notes that Isaiah does not read like second-temple literature, lacks the sort of Persian loanwords he would expect in that setting, and is generally dated by scholars to the Assyrian-period context rather than to Josiah or later reworking. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IKcic5xC9fA?t=7434" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:03:54</a> | How old was David when he fought Goliath? | He says the exact age is not given, but David was clearly young and not yet a full adult warrior. | He says the text does not give an exact age, but David was below normal fighting age and not yet physically filled out enough to wear armor. He guesses roughly the middle teenage years, perhaps around fourteen or fifteen, while stressing that this is an inference from David being a shepherd, unable to handle the armor, but still capable of acting independently. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IKcic5xC9fA?t=7844" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:10:44</a> | Does Micah 5:2 prove the Messiah is divine or eternal in some way? | He says the passage points in that direction and treats the wording as theologically weighty. | He says Micah 5:2 at least indicates that the Messiah's pre-incarnate existence does not begin at his birth in Bethlehem. He emphasizes the language of "goings forth" from long ago and from the days of eternity, treating it as religiously charged language that contrasts an insignificant town with a ruler whose origin and activity reach into unfathomable antiquity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IKcic5xC9fA?t=8424" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:20:24</a> | What is theosis, and how does biblical theosis differ from Mormon theosis? | He says both speak of taking on Godlike attributes, but biblical theosis does not make humans independent gods. | He defines theosis as taking on Godlike attributes in the afterlife, with eternal life as the attribute all Christians in the debate agree the saved receive. He contrasts this with Mormon theosis, which he describes as becoming gods of one's own worlds in a more polytheistic framework, while biblical theosis keeps God as God of the universe and speaks instead of believers becoming conformed to Christ. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IKcic5xC9fA?t=9463" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:37:43</a> | Did Egyptians count bodies by taking ears or hands? | He says they took right hands, the sword hand, and points to depictions such as Medinet Habu. | He says Egyptians counted by taking right hands, since each enemy would have only one sword hand. He points to scenes such as Medinet Habu where scribes are shown tabulating piles of hands, and also mentions a pit at Avaris containing hands as evidence that this was a literal practice. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IKcic5xC9fA?t=9713" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:41:53</a> | Does monarchal monotheism imply divinity besides God of a kind? | He says it allows a hierarchy of lesser divine beings while maintaining one supreme God. | He says monarchal monotheism does allow a kind of divinity below God, but not the same kind of divinity God has. Using Egyptian ntr and Hebrew elohim as analogies, he describes one supreme, self-sufficient God above dependent created beings such as angels, powers, and other spirits. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IKcic5xC9fA?t=10144" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:49:04</a> | Would an ancient Greek nymph count as a nephesh? | He says yes, that would fit the category. | He gives a brief affirmative answer, saying a Greek nymph would fit the category being discussed. The transcript does not expand the point further before he moves to the next super chat and channel-support comments. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IKcic5xC9fA?t=10224" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:50:24</a> | By the Iron Age, how many Israelites were descendants of Jacob rather than assimilated people? | He says that is almost impossible to differentiate because assimilation and intermarriage both occurred. | He says the distinction is nearly impossible by the Iron Age because Israel was already a mixed people when leaving Egypt and then intermarried with Canaanites and Philistines in the land. He mentions a mixed multitude including groups such as Amorites, Libyans, Nubians, Egyptians, and possibly Shasu, while noting that some priestly or return-from-exile lines preserved descent records. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IKcic5xC9fA?t=10510" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:55:10</a> | What is the fourth watch? | He initially asks for clarification, then identifies it as the watch of the night in the gospel account of Jesus coming on the water. | After asking what the questioner means, he understands it as the fourth watch of the night when Jesus came to the disciples on the water. He says he would need to look up the exact hour and Gospel context in a commentary, so he defers rather than giving a confident technical answer. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IKcic5xC9fA?t=10622" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:57:02</a> | What are Dr. Falk's thoughts on Judaism originating from Canaanite gods? | He rejects that view and says he sees more traction in a Kenite-related origin hypothesis for Yahweh worship. | He says Judaism had intertextuality with Canaanite literature but does not think its ideas came from Canaanite gods. He finds the Kenite hypothesis more plausible: a core Abrahamic religion centered on El Shaddai is joined to the identity of Yahweh that Moses brings from Midian, though he says even a proven Canaanite connection would need to be evaluated by evidence rather than dismissed automatically. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IKcic5xC9fA?t=10729" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:58:49</a> | What books or scholarly sources does Dr. Falk recommend on dating the Exodus? | He recommends Kenneth Kitchen, James Hoffmeier, and his own Ark of the Covenant work as starting points. | He recommends Kenneth Kitchen and James Hoffmeier as the major starting points, and also mentions his own Ark of the Covenant book because it addresses the topic. For a seminary student whose professors disagree, he advises learning the assigned material for class while continuing to read the evidence and arguments independently for future teaching. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IKcic5xC9fA?t=12212" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:23:32</a> | Can Dr. Falk review the Dinesh D'Souza and Alex O'Connor debate and explain where D'Souza went wrong? | He says D'Souza was unprepared and not functioning as a serious apologist in that debate. | He says D'Souza was unprepared, lacked training in theology, apologetics, and the ancient Near East, and should not have been presented as an apologist in that debate. He treats the result as damaging because the apologetics community then had to respond after the fact to questions he says D'Souza had not seriously thought through. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IKcic5xC9fA?t=12617" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:30:17</a> | Why are experts not invited to serious public discussions like these? | He says many academics will not accept such invitations, and some debate formats prefer punching down rather than expert engagement. | He gives two reasons: many academics will not accept those invitations, and many public debate formats are arranged to favor weaker opponents rather than expert engagement. He argues that online skeptics and public figures have audience and income incentives to choose opponents carefully, because losing debates can affect their support. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IKcic5xC9fA?t=13076" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:37:56</a> | Is there biblical or extrabiblical evidence that some Black people are related to Israelites in the Bible? | He says generally not, while noting the answer depends on definitions and on the Semitic context of ancient Israel. | He answers generally no, depending on how "Black" is defined, because Israelites were largely a Semitic or Afro-Semitic population. He does say some Nubians, Kushites, and other sub-Saharan Africans could have been incorporated through the mixed multitude, but their phenotypes would have been a minority and largely absorbed through intermarriage. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IKcic5xC9fA?t=13237" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:40:37</a> | How do you know you are being called for ministry? | He says to look for both an internal desire and external recognition or confirmation. | He describes two forms of calling: an eager passion for ministry, and a "kicking and screaming" call where God closes other paths and pushes a reluctant person toward ministry. His clearest test is whether the person "can do no other," either because their passion for God and the lost permits no other path or because circumstances make obedience the only viable path. |
